title: Emergency Guide: Dog Hit by Car and Limping
meta_desc: If your dog was hit by a car and is limping, this is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care and triage photos are critical.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog is limping after being hit by a car, it is a high-urgency emergency requiring immediate veterinary attention to rule out internal bleeding or organ damage. Adrenaline often masks severe pain, so prompt professional evaluation is vital even if your pet seems stable.

What does it mean if my dog is limping after being hit by a car?

When a dog is hit by a vehicle and exhibits limping, it signifies a traumatic injury to the musculoskeletal system. While the limp itself may indicate a fracture, ligament tear, or severe bruising, it is often a visible symptom of a much larger impact. In veterinary medicine, 'Hit by Car' (HBC) cases are treated with extreme caution because the force of the impact affects the entire body, not just the limb.

Is it an emergency if my dog is limping after a vehicle accident?

  • High-Urgency Emergency: This is a critical situation. Even if your dog is conscious and the limp appears minor, they may be suffering from life-threatening internal conditions such as internal bleeding, a collapsed lung (pneumothorax), or ruptured internal organs.
  • The Role of Adrenaline: Adrenaline can mask severe pain and shock, making a dog appear 'fine' initially when they are actually in critical condition.
  • Immediate Action: Immediate transport to an emergency veterinarian is required.

How can photos or videos help the veterinary team triage my dog?

  • Visual Assessment: If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, taking a quick photo of any visible wounds or a short video of how your dog is moving can be incredibly helpful.
  • Advance Preparation: These visuals allow the veterinary triage team to assess the severity of the trauma and prepare the necessary equipment—such as oxygen, pain relief, or surgical kits—before you even walk through the door.
  • Priority Warning: Never prioritize photography over the safety and immediate transport of your pet.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In a dog hit by a car and limping, stabilize any fractures below the elbow or stifle with significant displacement using a temporary splint made from a rolled newspaper or magazine secured with fabric or tape to prevent further damage to muscle, nerve, vessels, or bone during transport. Control any arterial bleeding with direct digital pressure followed by a pressure bandage. If bleeding is venous, apply a pressure bandage. Animals with altered mentation after trauma should be transported with the head level with the body or elevated 20°, avoiding any jerking or thrashing.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my dog to the vet?
faqs.content: Keep your dog as still as possible. Use a flat board, a sturdy box, or a thick blanket as a makeshift stretcher to prevent further injury to the spine or limbs. Keep them warm to help combat shock.

faqs.question: My dog is walking now, should I still go to the emergency room?
faqs.content: Yes. Internal injuries and internal bleeding often do not show symptoms immediately. A professional evaluation is the only way to ensure there are no hidden life-threatening issues.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication for the limp?
faqs.content: No. Never administer human medications like ibuprofen, aspirin, or Tylenol to a dog, especially after a trauma. These can be toxic and may interfere with the emergency medications the veterinarian needs to use.
